user_request: Draft a suit for recovery of Rs.20,00,000/- paid as advance for a business transaction which failed due to Defendant's default. Plead total failure of consideration under Section 65 of Indian Contract Act. Claim refund with interest and costs. Include cause of action paragraph with accrual date, continuing nature of cause of action, valuation, court fee, and proper verification clause. Draft suitable for filing before the District Court.

facts.summary: Plaintiff seeks recovery of Rs. 20,00,000 paid as advance for a business transaction that failed due to the Defendant's default. The suit claims total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, seeking refund with interest and costs.

assumptions: Jurisdiction lies with District Court based on valuation or cause of action

sections.body:
SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

sections.heading: BETWEEN
sections.instruction: Draft the parties section listing plaintiff and defendant(s) with full particulars: name, age, occupation, and address. Use sui juris averment for competency. If any detail is missing, use the appropriate {{PLACEHOLDER}}.

sections.heading: JURISDICTION
sections.instruction: Draft the jurisdiction section establishing territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction. For territorial jurisdiction, state SPECIFIC facts: where the Defendant resides or carries on business, where payment was made, where the agreement was executed, or where the cause of action arose — with concrete place references. For pecuniary jurisdiction, state the claim amount and that it falls within this court's monetary limits.

sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
sections.instruction: Draft the facts section as numbered paragraphs in chronological narrative form. Each paragraph must tell a story — not state conclusions. Cover: (1) How the parties came to know each other and the nature of their relationship, (2) What was agreed — the specific terms of the transaction/agreement, (3) What obligations each party undertook and the timeline for performance, (4) How payment was made — specific amount, mode, date, (5) What exactly constituted the defendant's failure — describe the specific default with detail, (6) What happened after the default — communications, demands, legal notice and response. Reference documents as 'Annexure [LABEL]'. Do NOT state legal conclusions in this section — keep it purely factual.

sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
sections.instruction: Draft the legal basis section in paragraph form. For each statutory provision: (1) State what the provision says — the legal rule it creates, (2) Explain what factual conditions trigger its application, (3) Show how the facts of this case satisfy those conditions. If applicable, include an alternative plea (e.g., Section 73 ICA as alternative to Section 65 ICA) using 'In the alternative and without prejudice to the above...' framing. Include defensive pleading: pre-emptively neutralize likely defense arguments (e.g., no part performance was rendered, no forfeiture clause exists, no adjustment made, no counter-claim exists). Cite ONLY provisions from the verified list.

must_include.description: At least one statutory section cited
Legal consequence explained (not just section named)

sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
sections.instruction: Draft the cause of action section in PARAGRAPH prose (not numbered sub-headings). Must cover: (1) ORIGIN — when and how the right to sue first arose, anchored to the default/failure date, (2) FURTHER ACCRUAL — when the cause of action further arose (e.g., failure to comply with legal notice), (3) CONTINUITY — that the cause of action is continuing because the defendant continues to wrongfully retain the money. The cause must be anchored to the failure/default date, NOT the notice date. Write in flowing paragraph form — avoid mechanical sub-headings like '(a) underlying obligation'.

sections.heading: LIMITATION
sections.instruction: Draft the limitation section stating: (1) the applicable Article of the Limitation Act, 1963 Schedule (from enrichment or use placeholder), (2) the limitation period, (3) the starting date of limitation (must be anchored to cause_of_action_date/default_date, NOT notice_date), (4) that the suit is within time. If limitation article is not available in enrichment, use {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} placeholder.

sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
sections.instruction: Draft the valuation and court fee section stating: (1) suit valuation equals the claim amount, (2) court fee calculated per applicable state court fees act. Use court_fee context from web search if available, otherwise use {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} placeholder.

sections.heading: INTEREST
sections.instruction: Draft the interest section with a 3-part structure: (1) Pre-suit interest — from default date to filing date with rate and justification, (2) Pendente lite interest — during pendency of suit under Section 34 CPC with rate, (3) Future interest — post-decree until realization with rate. Justify the interest rate: state why the claimed rate is appropriate (commercial transaction, wrongful retention, prevailing bank rate, contractual understanding). Cite Section 34 of CPC for pendente lite interest.

sections.heading: PRAYER
sections.instruction: Draft the prayer section with at least 6 specific sub-prayers as lettered items (a, b, c, ...). Each prayer must state exact amounts or rate placeholders — never vague. Must include: (a) decree for principal amount with Rs. figure, (b) pre-suit interest with rate and date range, (c) pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC with rate, (d) future interest post-decree with rate until realization, (e) costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs for sending legal notice, (f) any other relief the court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances.

sections.heading: LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON
sections.instruction: Generate a numbered list of documents the plaintiff relies upon, mapping to the annexure scheme. For each document: Serial No., Description, Annexure Label. Only list documents mentioned in evidence. If evidence is empty, list standard expected documents with {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}} placeholders.

sections.heading: VERIFICATION
sections.body:
I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of the above plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and the contents based on legal advice are believed to be true on the basis of such advice. No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

filled_sections.heading: BETWEEN
filled_sections.text: The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, and employed as {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, is sui juris and competent to sue in their own name. The Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, and employed as {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, is sui juris and capable of defending this action.

filled_sections.heading: JURISDICTION
filled_sections.text: The Territorial and Pecuniary Jurisdiction of the District Court is invoked in this suit. The Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which lies within the territorial jurisdiction of this Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}}. The advance payment of Rs. 2,000,000 was transferred from the Plaintiff’s account held with {{BANK_NAME}}, {{BANK_BRANCH}} on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} and was received by the Defendant at its place of business located at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}. The Agreement was executed on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} at {{AGREEMENT_LOCATION}} in relation to which the Plaintiff advanced the sum as consideration for a business transaction. The cause of action in this matter first arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} when the Defendant defaulted in performing its obligations, resulting in a total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act. This Court is the proper forum for the dispute because the facts bring the matter squarely within its territorial reach, and the relief sought pertains to a monetary claim arising from the agreement and subsequent default. Pecuniary jurisdiction is satisfied as the Plaintiff seeks Rs. 2,000,000 (Rupees Two Million), which falls within this Court’s monetary limits for civil suits. Place: {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}}.

filled_sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
filled_sections.text:
1. In or about {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} at {{PLACE_NAME}}, Plaintiff and Defendant engaged in discussions regarding a prospective business transaction and, as a result of those discussions, entered into a written Agreement (Annexure A). The relationship between Plaintiff and Defendant arose out of these negotiations and was framed as a commercial arrangement wherein Plaintiff agreed to provide an advance to Defendant for the purposes of the contemplated transaction, with the terms and conditions set out in Annexure A. 

2. The terms of the Agreement (Annexure A) dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} contemplated that the Plaintiff would make an advance of Rs. 20,00,000 (Rs. 2,000,000) to the Defendant in consideration of the Defendant performing the agreed transaction and delivering the prescribed outputs by the specified timeline, and that the Defendant would undertake to complete the transaction and furnish the outputs by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}. The Agreement also outlined the manner in which the advance would be utilized and the conditions under which the transaction would proceed, as evidenced by Annexure A.

3. The obligations under the Agreement were that the Plaintiff would advance Rs. 20,00,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} to support the Defendant’s performance of the transaction, while the Defendant undertook to perform the agreed actions and deliver the outputs by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}, thereby concluding the transaction as contemplated in Annexure A. The Parties’ respective duties, timeline for performance, and the expected deliverables were all described within the Agreement, as reflected in Annexure A.

4. Payment was effected when, on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff remitted Rs. 20,00,000 to the Defendant, the transfer being supported by payment proof in Annexure B. The funds were directed to the Defendant’s designated account, and the transaction was recorded in the Plaintiff’s and Defendant’s financial records as evidenced by Annexure B.

5. On {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant failed to perform the promised obligations by the agreed due date and did not furnish the outputs or deliver the consideration promised under Annexure A. The funds advanced by the Plaintiff remained with the Defendant without the promised return or performance, and the Defendant offered no timely or adequate justification for the non-performance, despite the Plaintiff’s expectations and the terms of Annexure A.

6. After the default, the Plaintiff sent a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} demanding repayment of Rs. 20,00,000 and requesting compliance with the terms of the Agreement; the notice is contained in Annexure C. The Defendant responded to the legal notice, with communication dated {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} (Annexure D), which did not rectify the non-performance. Thereafter, the Plaintiff filed the present suit on {{FILING_DATE}} to recover the amount advanced along with costs and interest as permitted. Annexure F contains any additional supporting documents, if relevant, and Annexure E would contain any acknowledgment/admission, if such exists.

filled_sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
filled_sections.text:
Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 creates a legal obligation to restore any amount or advantage received under an agreement or contract that is void or which becomes void, to the person from whom it was received, where the consideration fails or the contract collapses. In the present case, the Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, paid Rs. 2,000,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} to the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, in pursuance of the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} (Annexure A) executed at {{PLACE}} for a business transaction, and the payment is supported by Annexure B (payment proof). The Defendant undertook to perform the agreed obligations by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} under the terms of the contract, but by {{DEFAULT_DATE}} it failed to repay or perform, resulting in a total failure of consideration. The Defendant has retained the Rs. 2,000,000 without any lawful justification, thereby triggering the restoration obligation under Section 65, and the Plaintiff is entitled to restitution of Rs. 2,000,000, with interest and costs calculated from the date of default. This recovery is framed as refund/restitution for the value of money advanced in connection with the void or voidable contract evidenced by Annexures A and B, and it is further supported by the correspondence and course of events reflected in Annexure C (legal notice) and Annexure D (reply, if any). The present pleading anticipates that the Defendant may attempt to argue that part performance or adjustment exists; however, the facts show complete or total failure of consideration with no beneficial performance rendered by the Defendant in exchange for the advance, and there is no forfeiture clause in Annexure A, no counter-claim raised, and no contractual set-off that would defeat restitution; accordingly, the Plaintiff seeks the full amount of Rs. 2,000,000 with interest and costs as the legal consequence of the failure of consideration and the restoration duty under the statute. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, if the Court finds that Section 65 cannot be invoked in the precise form urged, the Plaintiff pleads under Article 47 of the Limitation Act, 1963 that the suit is filed within three years from the accrual of the cause of action, which accrued on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} in light of the Defendant’s default on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} and the culmination of the contract’s failure; the filing on {{FILING_DATE}} is within the period prescribed by Article 47, and the Plaintiff seeks the same refund with interest and costs. Annexures A, B, and C evidence the agreement, payment, and legal notice respectively, and Annexure D records any reply. The relief sought is restitution of the amount advanced and the concomitant costs, with interest as permitted by law. 
---SECTION_TEXT---

filled_sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
filled_sections.text:
The cause of action of the Plaintiff against the Defendant first arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to repay the Rs. 20,00,000 advanced by the Plaintiff and to perform the contractual obligations under the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} for the business transaction, the Plaintiff having paid Rs. 20,00,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} as consideration, as evidenced by Annexure A (Agreement/Contract) and Annexure B (Payment proof). This constitutes total failure of consideration within the meaning of Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, and the Plaintiff's right to sue for refund accrued at that time. The cause of action further accrued on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Plaintiff served the legal notice demanding refund, the notice being supported by Annexure C, but the Defendant, having received the notice, refused to repay or remedy the breach, thereby continuing to withhold the amount. The cause of action is a continuing one because the Defendant continues to wrongfully retain the Plaintiff's sum of Rs. 20,00,000 without any lawful authority or justification, and the retention persists to the date of filing and shall continue until actual realization, with the Plaintiff seeking refund of Rs. 20,00,000 along with interest and costs.
---SECTION_TEXT---

filled_sections.heading: LIMITATION
filled_sections.text:
The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article 47 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963. Article 47 provides a period of three years for money paid upon an existing consideration which afterwards fails, and the accrual date is the date of the failure. The cause of action first arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}}, being the date the defendant defaulted in repayment or performance, i.e., {{DEFAULT_DATE}}. Accordingly, the three-year period commenced on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} and the suit was filed on {{FILING_DATE}}, within three years from the date of accrual. The present suit is therefore within time.
---SECTION_TEXT---

filled_sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
filled_sections.text: The suit valuation is Rs. 2,000,000, being the principal amount claimed by the Plaintiff against the Defendant for recovery. The court fee payable on this suit is calculated under the applicable state court fees act; the amount of court fee due is Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}.

filled_sections.heading: INTEREST
filled_sections.text:
Pre-suit interest: The Plaintiff claims pre-suit interest on the principal sum of Rs. 2,000,000 at the rate of 12% per annum from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}, calculated on the basis of a commercial/mercantile rate justified by the commercial transaction between the parties and the contractual understanding evidenced by Annexure A (Agreement/Contract) dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}. The Plaintiff advanced Rs. 2,000,000 to the Defendant under the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} (Annexure A) and the Defendant failed to repay the amount on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, thereby depriving the Plaintiff of the use of funds and rendering pre-suit interest appropriate as a compensation for the wrongful retention of money under the terms of the contract and prevailing mercantile practice. The payment proof reflecting the advance is evidenced by Annexure B.

Pendente lite interest: The Plaintiff claims pendente lite interest at the rate of 12% per annum on Rs. 2,000,000 for the period from {{FILING_DATE}} to {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} under Section 34 of the CPC, which governs interest during the pendency of a money claim. The legal basis rests on the commercial nature of the transaction and the Defendant’s wrongful retention of funds after default, which deprived the Plaintiff of the use of its money during the litigation. The rate is justified by commercial/mercantile norms applicable to such transactions and by the contractual understanding between the parties, as evidenced by Annexure A, with the advance and related documents in Annexure B supporting the claim.

Future interest: The Plaintiff seeks post-decree interest at 12% per annum on the principal sum from the date of decree until realisation, justified by the prevailing mercantile practice and the contractual understanding between the parties. The decree will set out the amount payable, and the Plaintiff should be entitled to interest on such amount from the date of decree until realisation in accordance with Section 34 CPC, until actual recovery. The rate aligns with commercial norms and the need to compensate for the delay in realisation, as contemplated by the same contractual framework and the underlying agreement (Annexure A) and payment evidence (Annexure B).

Cited statutory basis: Section 34 CPC is invoked to address pendente lite interest on money due and payable during the litigation, including the post-decree phase, to the extent permitted by law and the evidence on record. The justification for the chosen rate in all three periods rests on the commercial character of the transaction, the Defendant’s wrongful retention of funds, and the prevailing mercantile/ bank-rate context applicable to such recovery actions, in harmony with the contractual understanding reflected in Annexure A.

filled_sections.heading: PRAYER
filled_sections.text:
(a) Pass a decree directing the Defendant to pay to the Plaintiff a sum of Rs. 2,000,000/- (Rupees Two Million Only) being the principal amount advanced under the contract dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}. 
(b) Direct the Defendant to pay pre-suit interest at the rate of {{PRE_SUIT_RATE}}% per annum on the said principal sum from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}. 
(c) Award pendente lite interest on the said debt at the rate of {{Pendente_Lite_RATE}}% per annum under Section 34 CPC from {{FILING_DATE}} till the date of decree. 
(d) Direct that after the decree, the Defendant pay future interest at the rate of {{FUTURE_RATE}}% per annum on the decretal amount from the date of decree until realization. 
(e) Award costs of the suit to the Plaintiff including pre-litigation costs for sending the legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} and postal charges, amounting to Rs. {{TOTAL_COSTS}}/-. 
(f) Pass any other relief the Court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

draft_artifacts.title: SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
draft_artifacts.text:
IN THE COURT OF THE {{COURT_NAME}}
AT {{COURT_PLACE}}

O.S. No. {{SUIT_NUMBER}} / {{SUIT_YEAR}}

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)


BETWEEN

The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, and employed as {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, is sui juris and competent to sue in their own name. The Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, and employed as {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, is sui juris and capable of defending this action.


JURISDICTION

The Territorial and Pecuniary Jurisdiction of the District Court is invoked in this suit. The Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which lies within the territorial jurisdiction of this Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}}. The advance payment of Rs. 2,000,000 was transferred from the Plaintiff’s account held with {{BANK_NAME}}, {{BANK_BRANCH}} on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} and was received by the Defendant at its place of business located at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}. The Agreement was executed on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} at {{AGREEMENT_LOCATION}} in relation to which the Plaintiff advanced the sum as consideration for a business transaction. The cause of action in this matter first arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} when the Defendant defaulted in performing its obligations, resulting in a total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act. This Court is the proper forum for the dispute because the facts bring the matter squarely within its territorial reach, and the relief sought pertains to a monetary claim arising from the agreement and subsequent default. Pecuniary jurisdiction is satisfied as the Plaintiff seeks Rs. 2,000,000 (Rupees Two Million), which falls within this Court’s monetary limits for civil suits. Place: {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}}.


FACTS OF THE CASE

1. In or about {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} at {{PLACE_NAME}}, Plaintiff and Defendant engaged in discussions regarding a prospective business transaction and, as a result of those discussions, entered into a written Agreement (Annexure A). The relationship between Plaintiff and Defendant arose out of these negotiations and was framed as a commercial arrangement wherein Plaintiff agreed to provide an advance to Defendant for the purposes of the contemplated transaction, with the terms and conditions set out in Annexure A. 

2. The terms of the Agreement (Annexure A) dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} contemplated that the Plaintiff would make an advance of Rs. 20,00,000 (Rs. 2,000,000) to the Defendant in consideration of the Defendant performing the agreed transaction and delivering the prescribed outputs by the specified timeline, and that the Defendant would undertake to complete the transaction and furnish the outputs by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}. The Agreement also outlined the manner in which the advance would be utilized and the conditions under which the transaction would proceed, as evidenced by Annexure A.

3. The obligations under the Agreement were that the Plaintiff would advance Rs. 20,00,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} to support the Defendant’s performance of the transaction, while the Defendant undertook to perform the agreed actions and deliver the outputs by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}, thereby concluding the transaction as contemplated in Annexure A. The Parties’ respective duties, timeline for performance, and the expected deliverables were all described within the Agreement, as reflected in Annexure A.

4. Payment was effected when, on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff remitted Rs. 20,00,000 to the Defendant, the transfer being supported by payment proof in Annexure B. The funds were directed to the Defendant’s designated account, and the transaction was recorded in the Plaintiff’s and Defendant’s financial records as evidenced by Annexure B.

5. On {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant failed to perform the promised obligations by the agreed due date and did not furnish the outputs or deliver the consideration promised under Annexure A. The funds advanced by the Plaintiff remained with the Defendant without the promised return or performance, and the Defendant offered no timely or adequate justification for the non-performance, despite the Plaintiff’s expectations and the terms of Annexure A.

6. After the default, the Plaintiff sent a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} demanding repayment of Rs. 20,00,000 and requesting compliance with the terms of the Agreement; the notice is contained in Annexure C. The Defendant responded to the legal notice, with communication dated {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} (Annexure D), which did not rectify the non-performance. Thereafter, the Plaintiff filed the present suit on {{FILING_DATE}} to recover the amount advanced along with costs and interest as permitted. Annexure F contains any additional supporting documents, if relevant, and Annexure E would contain any acknowledgment/admission, if such exists.


LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 creates a legal obligation to restore any amount or advantage received under an agreement or contract that is void or which becomes void, to the person from whom it was received, where the consideration fails or the contract collapses. In the present case, the Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, paid Rs. 2,000,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} to the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, in pursuance of the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} (Annexure A) executed at {{PLACE}} for a business transaction, and the payment is supported by Annexure B (payment proof). The Defendant undertook to perform the agreed obligations by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} under the terms of the contract, but by {{DEFAULT_DATE}} it failed to repay or perform, resulting in a total failure of consideration. The Defendant has retained the Rs. 2,000,000 without any lawful justification, thereby triggering the restoration obligation under Section 65, and the Plaintiff is entitled to restitution of Rs. 2,000,000, with interest and costs calculated from the date of default. This recovery is framed as refund/restitution for the value of money advanced in connection with the void or voidable contract evidenced by Annexures A and B, and it is further supported by the correspondence and course of events reflected in Annexure C (legal notice) and Annexure D (reply, if any). The present pleading anticipates that the Defendant may attempt to argue that part performance or adjustment exists; however, the facts show complete or total failure of consideration with no beneficial performance rendered by the Defendant in exchange for the advance, and there is no forfeiture clause in Annexure A, no counter-claim raised, and no contractual set-off that would defeat restitution; accordingly, the Plaintiff seeks the full amount of Rs. 2,000,000 with interest and costs as the legal consequence of the failure of consideration and the restoration duty under the statute. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, if the Court finds that Section 65 cannot be invoked in the precise form urged, the Plaintiff pleads under Article 47 of the Limitation Act, 1963 that the suit is filed within three years from the accrual of the cause of action, which accrued on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} in light of the Defendant’s default on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} and the culmination of the contract’s failure; the filing on {{FILING_DATE}} is within the period prescribed by Article 47, and the Plaintiff seeks the same refund with interest and costs. Annexures A, B, and C evidence the agreement, payment, and legal notice respectively, and Annexure D records any reply. The relief sought is restitution of the amount advanced and the concomitant costs, with interest as permitted by law. 

---SECTION_TEXT---


CAUSE OF ACTION

The cause of action of the Plaintiff against the Defendant first arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to repay the Rs. 20,00,000 advanced by the Plaintiff and to perform the contractual obligations under the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} for the business transaction, the Plaintiff having paid Rs. 20,00,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} as consideration, as evidenced by Annexure A (Agreement/Contract) and Annexure B (Payment proof). This constitutes total failure of consideration within the meaning of Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, and the Plaintiff's right to sue for refund accrued at that time. The cause of action further accrued on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Plaintiff served the legal notice demanding refund, the notice being supported by Annexure C, but the Defendant, having received the notice, refused to repay or remedy the breach, thereby continuing to withhold the amount. The cause of action is a continuing one because the Defendant continues to wrongfully retain the Plaintiff's sum of Rs. 20,00,000 without any lawful authority or justification, and the retention persists to the date of filing and shall continue until actual realization, with the Plaintiff seeking refund of Rs. 20,00,000 along with interest and costs.

---SECTION_TEXT---


LIMITATION

The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article 47 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963. Article 47 provides a period of three years for money paid upon an existing consideration which afterwards fails, and the accrual date is the date of the failure. The cause of action first arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}}, being the date the defendant defaulted in repayment or performance, i.e., {{DEFAULT_DATE}}. Accordingly, the three-year period commenced on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} and the suit was filed on {{FILING_DATE}}, within three years from the date of accrual. The present suit is therefore within time.

---SECTION_TEXT---


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

The suit valuation is Rs. 2,000,000, being the principal amount claimed by the Plaintiff against the Defendant for recovery. The court fee payable on this suit is calculated under the applicable state court fees act; the amount of court fee due is Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}.


INTEREST

Pre-suit interest: The Plaintiff claims pre-suit interest on the principal sum of Rs. 2,000,000 at the rate of 12% per annum from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}, calculated on the basis of a commercial/mercantile rate justified by the commercial transaction between the parties and the contractual understanding evidenced by Annexure A (Agreement/Contract) dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}. The Plaintiff advanced Rs. 2,000,000 to the Defendant under the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} (Annexure A) and the Defendant failed to repay the amount on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, thereby depriving the Plaintiff of the use of funds and rendering pre-suit interest appropriate as a compensation for the wrongful retention of money under the terms of the contract and prevailing mercantile practice. The payment proof reflecting the advance is evidenced by Annexure B.

Pendente lite interest: The Plaintiff claims pendente lite interest at the rate of 12% per annum on Rs. 2,000,000 for the period from {{FILING_DATE}} to {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} under Section 34 of the CPC, which governs interest during the pendency of a money claim. The legal basis rests on the commercial nature of the transaction and the Defendant’s wrongful retention of funds after default, which deprived the Plaintiff of the use of its money during the litigation. The rate is justified by commercial/mercantile norms applicable to such transactions and by the contractual understanding between the parties, as evidenced by Annexure A, with the advance and related documents in Annexure B supporting the claim.

Future interest: The Plaintiff seeks post-decree interest at 12% per annum on the principal sum from the date of decree until realisation, justified by the prevailing mercantile practice and the contractual understanding between the parties. The decree will set out the amount payable, and the Plaintiff should be entitled to interest on such amount from the date of decree until realisation in accordance with Section 34 CPC, until actual recovery. The rate aligns with commercial norms and the need to compensate for the delay in realisation, as contemplated by the same contractual framework and the underlying agreement (Annexure A) and payment evidence (Annexure B).

Cited statutory basis: Section 34 CPC is invoked to address pendente lite interest on money due and payable during the litigation, including the post-decree phase, to the extent permitted by law and the evidence on record. The justification for the chosen rate in all three periods rests on the commercial character of the transaction, the Defendant’s wrongful retention of funds, and the prevailing mercantile/ bank-rate context applicable to such recovery actions, in harmony with the contractual understanding reflected in Annexure A.


PRAYER

(a) Pass a decree directing the Defendant to pay to the Plaintiff a sum of Rs. 2,000,000/- (Rupees Two Million Only) being the principal amount advanced under the contract dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}. 
(b) Direct the Defendant to pay pre-suit interest at the rate of {{PRE_SUIT_RATE}}% per annum on the said principal sum from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}. 
(c) Award pendente lite interest on the said debt at the rate of {{Pendente_Lite_RATE}}% per annum under Section 34 CPC from {{FILING_DATE}} till the date of decree. 
(d) Direct that after the decree, the Defendant pay future interest at the rate of {{FUTURE_RATE}}% per annum on the decretal amount from the date of decree until realization. 
(e) Award costs of the suit to the Plaintiff including pre-litigation costs for sending the legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} and postal charges, amounting to Rs. {{TOTAL_COSTS}}/-. 
(f) Pass any other relief the Court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.


LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON

7. Serial No. 1, Description: {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}}, Annexure Label: Annexure A.
8. Serial No. 2, Description: {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}}, Annexure Label: Annexure B.
9. Serial No. 3, Description: {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}}, Annexure Label: Annexure C.
10. Serial No. 4, Description: {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}}, Annexure Label: Annexure D.
11. Serial No. 5, Description: {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}}, Annexure Label: Annexure E.
12. Serial No. 6, Description: {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}}, Annexure Label: Annexure F.


VERIFICATION

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of the above plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and the contents based on legal advice are believed to be true on the basis of such advice. No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

final_artifacts.title: SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
final_artifacts.text:
IN THE COURT OF THE {{COURT_NAME}}
AT {{COURT_PLACE}}

O.S. No. {{SUIT_NUMBER}} / {{SUIT_YEAR}}

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)


BETWEEN

The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, and employed as {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, is sui juris and competent to sue in their own name. The Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, and employed as {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, is sui juris and capable of defending this action.


JURISDICTION

The Territorial and Pecuniary Jurisdiction of the District Court is invoked in this suit. The Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which lies within the territorial jurisdiction of this Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}}. The advance payment of Rs. 2,000,000 was transferred from the Plaintiff’s account held with {{BANK_NAME}}, {{BANK_BRANCH}} on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} and was received by the Defendant at its place of business located at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}. The Agreement was executed on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} at {{AGREEMENT_LOCATION}} in relation to which the Plaintiff advanced the sum as consideration for a business transaction. The cause of action in this matter first arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} when the Defendant defaulted in performing its obligations, resulting in a total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act. This Court is the proper forum for the dispute because the facts bring the matter squarely within its territorial reach, and the relief sought pertains to a monetary claim arising from the agreement and subsequent default. Pecuniary jurisdiction is satisfied as the Plaintiff seeks Rs. 2,000,000 (Rupees Two Million), which falls within this Court’s monetary limits for civil suits. Place: {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}}.


FACTS OF THE CASE

1. In or about {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} at {{PLACE_NAME}}, Plaintiff and Defendant engaged in discussions regarding a prospective business transaction and, as a result of those discussions, entered into a written Agreement (Annexure A). The relationship between Plaintiff and Defendant arose out of these negotiations and was framed as a commercial arrangement wherein Plaintiff agreed to provide an advance to Defendant for the purposes of the contemplated transaction, with the terms and conditions set out in Annexure A. 

2. The terms of the Agreement (Annexure A) dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} contemplated that the Plaintiff would make an advance of Rs. 20,00,000 (Rs. 2,000,000) to the Defendant in consideration of the Defendant performing the agreed transaction and delivering the prescribed outputs by the specified timeline, and that the Defendant would undertake to complete the transaction and furnish the outputs by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}. The Agreement also outlined the manner in which the advance would be utilized and the conditions under which the transaction would proceed, as evidenced by Annexure A.

3. The obligations under the Agreement were that the Plaintiff would advance Rs. 20,00,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} to support the Defendant’s performance of the transaction, while the Defendant undertook to perform the agreed actions and deliver the outputs by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}, thereby concluding the transaction as contemplated in Annexure A. The Parties’ respective duties, timeline for performance, and the expected deliverables were all described within the Agreement, as reflected in Annexure A.

4. Payment was effected when, on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff remitted Rs. 20,00,000 to the Defendant, the transfer being supported by payment proof in Annexure B. The funds were directed to the Defendant’s designated account, and the transaction was recorded in the Plaintiff’s and Defendant’s financial records as evidenced by Annexure B.

5. On {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant failed to perform the promised obligations by the agreed due date and did not furnish the outputs or deliver the consideration promised under Annexure A. The funds advanced by the Plaintiff remained with the Defendant without the promised return or performance, and the Defendant offered no timely or adequate justification for the non-performance, despite the Plaintiff’s expectations and the terms of Annexure A.

6. After the default, the Plaintiff sent a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} demanding repayment of Rs. 20,00,000 and requesting compliance with the terms of the Agreement; the notice is contained in Annexure C. The Defendant responded to the legal notice, with communication dated {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} (Annexure D), which did not rectify the non-performance. Thereafter, the Plaintiff filed the present suit on {{FILING_DATE}} to recover the amount advanced along with costs and interest as permitted. Annexure F contains any additional supporting documents, if relevant, and Annexure E would contain any acknowledgment/admission, if such exists.


LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 creates a legal obligation to restore any amount or advantage received under an agreement or contract that is void or which becomes void, to the person from whom it was received, where the consideration fails or the contract collapses. In the present case, the Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, paid Rs. 2,000,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} to the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, in pursuance of the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} (Annexure A) executed at {{PLACE}} for a business transaction, and the payment is supported by Annexure B (payment proof). The Defendant undertook to perform the agreed obligations by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} under the terms of the contract, but by {{DEFAULT_DATE}} it failed to repay or perform, resulting in a total failure of consideration. The Defendant has retained the Rs. 2,000,000 without any lawful justification, thereby triggering the restoration obligation under Section 65, and the Plaintiff is entitled to restitution of Rs. 2,000,000, with interest and costs calculated from the date of default. This recovery is framed as refund/restitution for the value of money advanced in connection with the void or voidable contract evidenced by Annexures A and B, and it is further supported by the correspondence and course of events reflected in Annexure C (legal notice) and Annexure D (reply, if any). The present pleading anticipates that the Defendant may attempt to argue that part performance or adjustment exists; however, the facts show complete or total failure of consideration with no beneficial performance rendered by the Defendant in exchange for the advance, and there is no forfeiture clause in Annexure A, no counter-claim raised, and no contractual set-off that would defeat restitution; accordingly, the Plaintiff seeks the full amount of Rs. 2,000,000 with interest and costs as the legal consequence of the failure of consideration and the restoration duty under the statute. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, if the Court finds that Section 65 cannot be invoked in the precise form urged, the Plaintiff pleads under Article 47 of the Limitation Act, 1963 that the suit is filed within three years from the accrual of the cause of action, which accrued on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} in light of the Defendant’s default on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} and the culmination of the contract’s failure; the filing on {{FILING_DATE}} is within the period prescribed by Article 47, and the Plaintiff seeks the same refund with interest and costs. Annexures A, B, and C evidence the agreement, payment, and legal notice respectively, and Annexure D records any reply. The relief sought is restitution of the amount advanced and the concomitant costs, with interest as permitted by law. 


CAUSE OF ACTION

The cause of action of the Plaintiff against the Defendant first arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to repay the Rs. 20,00,000 advanced by the Plaintiff and to perform the contractual obligations under the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} for the business transaction, the Plaintiff having paid Rs. 20,00,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} as consideration, as evidenced by Annexure A (Agreement/Contract) and Annexure B (Payment proof). This constitutes total failure of consideration within the meaning of Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, and the Plaintiff's right to sue for refund accrued at that time. The cause of action further accrued on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Plaintiff served the legal notice demanding refund, the notice being supported by Annexure C, but the Defendant, having received the notice, refused to repay or remedy the breach, thereby continuing to withhold the amount. The cause of action is a continuing one because the Defendant continues to wrongfully retain the Plaintiff's sum of Rs. 20,00,000 without any lawful authority or justification, and the retention persists to the date of filing and shall continue until actual realization, with the Plaintiff seeking refund of Rs. 20,00,000 along with interest and costs.


LIMITATION

The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article 47 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963. Article 47 provides a period of three years for money paid upon an existing consideration which afterwards fails, and the accrual date is the date of the failure. The cause of action first arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}}, being the date the defendant defaulted in repayment or performance, i.e., {{DEFAULT_DATE}}. Accordingly, the three-year period commenced on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} and the suit was filed on {{FILING_DATE}}, within three years from the date of accrual. The present suit is therefore within time.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

The suit valuation is Rs. 2,000,000, being the principal amount claimed by the Plaintiff against the Defendant for recovery. The court fee payable on this suit is calculated under the applicable state court fees act; the amount of court fee due is Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}.


INTEREST

Pre-suit interest: The Plaintiff claims pre-suit interest on the principal sum of Rs. 2,000,000 at the rate of 12% per annum from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}, calculated on the basis of a commercial/mercantile rate justified by the commercial transaction between the parties and the contractual understanding evidenced by Annexure A (Agreement/Contract) dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}. The Plaintiff advanced Rs. 2,000,000 to the Defendant under the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} (Annexure A) and the Defendant failed to repay the amount on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, thereby depriving the Plaintiff of the use of funds and rendering pre-suit interest appropriate as a compensation for the wrongful retention of money under the terms of the contract and prevailing mercantile practice. The payment proof reflecting the advance is evidenced by Annexure B.

Pendente lite interest: The Plaintiff claims pendente lite interest at the rate of 12% per annum on Rs. 2,000,000 for the period from {{FILING_DATE}} till the date of decree under Section 34 of the CPC, which governs interest during the pendency of a money claim. The legal basis rests on the commercial nature of the transaction and the Defendant’s wrongful retention of funds after default, which deprived the Plaintiff of the use of its money during the litigation. The rate is justified by commercial/mercantile norms applicable to such transactions and by the contractual understanding between the parties, as evidenced by Annexure A, with the advance and related documents in Annexure B supporting the claim.

Future interest: The Plaintiff seeks post-decree interest at 12% per annum on the principal sum from the date of decree until realisation, justified by the prevailing mercantile practice and the contractual understanding between the parties. The decree will set out the amount payable, and the Plaintiff should be entitled to interest on such amount from the date of decree until realisation in accordance with Section 34 CPC, until actual recovery. The rate aligns with commercial norms and the need to compensate for the delay in realisation, as contemplated by the same contractual framework and the underlying agreement (Annexure A) and payment evidence (Annexure B).

Cited statutory basis: Section 34 CPC is invoked to address pendente lite interest on money due and payable during the litigation, including the post-decree phase, to the extent permitted by law and the evidence on record. The justification for the chosen rate in all three periods rests on the commercial character of the transaction, the Defendant’s wrongful retention of funds, and the prevailing mercantile/ bank-rate context applicable to such recovery actions, in harmony with the contractual understanding reflected in Annexure A.


PRAYER

(a) Pass a decree directing the Defendant to pay to the Plaintiff a sum of Rs. 2,000,000/- (Rupees Two Million Only) being the principal amount advanced under the contract dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}. 
(b) Direct the Defendant to pay pre-suit interest at the rate of {{PRE_SUIT_RATE}}% per annum on the said principal sum from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}. 
(c) Award pendente lite interest on the said debt at the rate of {{Pendente_Lite_RATE}}% per annum under Section 34 CPC from {{FILING_DATE}} till the date of decree. 
(d) Direct that after the decree, the Defendant pay future interest at the rate of {{FUTURE_RATE}}% per annum on the decretal amount from the date of decree until realization. 
(e) Award costs of the suit to the Plaintiff including pre-litigation costs for sending the legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} and postal charges, amounting to Rs. {{TOTAL_COSTS}}/-. 
(f) Pass any other relief the Court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.
